data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a structural inevitability caused by the hyper-specialization of palatial labor forces which eliminated local resilience; when a novel naval raiding technology (the galley) bypassed static land defenses, the specialized centers could not revert to subsistence farming fast enough to survive the siege.", "mechanism": "Resilience trade-off: Centuries of stability drove extreme division of labor (scribes, bronze-smiths, administrators) at the expense of generalized agrarian skills among the population. The introduction of fast, oared galleys allowed small, mobile groups to strike deep inland via rivers/coasts, disrupting harvest cycles. The specialized populations starved because they lacked the knowledge or tools to immediately return to autonomous subsistence, unlike the more generalized Egyptian peasantry.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Rural hinterlands", "Egypt", "Nomadic groups"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence of widespread, decentralized grain storage or dual-use (military/agricultural) toolkits within the palatial citadels dating to the late 13th century.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical angle focusing on labor specialization and military technology interaction as the primary driver, rather than external invasion or simple resource shortage."}
